misconfigurations.name: Hardcoded Credentials in Containers
misconfigurations.description:
Containers that embed passwords, API keys, or tokens expose sensitive secrets to anyone able to pull the image or gain
access to a running pod. These credentials often propagate across multiple environments through CI/CD and image
promotion pipelines, making revocation difficult. Attackers can extract secrets from either image layers or live
containers. Hardcoded credentials break all secret rotation processes and weaken incident response.
misconfigurations.impact:
Read-only access to images or code execution in the affected container allows for recovering hardcoded authentication materials.
Attackers can use them to impersonate CI systems, tamper with source code, access private repositories, escalate privileges
within the environment or laterally move to cloud. Build systems may be compromised, enabling widespread injection attacks.
Stolen non-expiring credentials provide persistent access across multiple stages of the SDLC.

_or: Read-only access to the image repository
Code execution in a container via any of the `workload_code_execution` technique.

misconfigurations.recommendations:
Store secrets outside the build environment using external secret managers such as HashiCorp Vault
or cloud-native key vault (e.g., AWS SecretsManager). Ensure applications retrieve secrets at runtime and keep them only
in memory, avoiding the filesystem or image layers. Enforce automated secret scanning across CI logs, build contexts
and image layers, and block builds if secrets are detected. Rotate all build-time credentials frequently, revoke long-lived tokens,
and ensure leaked credentials trigger immediate rotation and invalidation. Maintain strict access controls on CI/CD environments
and source code repositories to minimize the chance of credential exposure.

misconfigurations.name: Containers with Useful Binaries (curl, wget, netcat, bash)
misconfigurations.description:
Containers containing full Linux distributions or common administrative tools give attackers powerful built-in
capabilities without requiring any additional payloads. Utilities such as `curl`, `wget`, `bash`, `python`, or `netcat` enable
interactive shells, internal scanning, tunneling, or downloading secondary payloads. Distroless containers, by
contrast, sharply limit post-exploitation capabilities by removing these tools entirely. Tool-rich containers allow
attackers to “live off the land” within the workload. This makes intrusion stealthier and faster.
misconfigurations.impact:
Attackers gain immediate reconnaissance, tunneling, and exfiltration ability. They can contact internal or external
services, craft reverse shells, or download malware. Monitoring and incident response become harder to implement because actions
use built-in legitimate binaries. In addition, the exploitation speed increases significantly when the tools are already available
in the compromised container.
misconfigurations.prerequisites: Code execution in a container via any of the `workload_code_execution` technique.
misconfigurations.recommendations:
Use distroless or minimal-base images in production to remove unnecessary tooling. Strip shells, package managers,
compilers, and networking utilities from runtime workloads and ensure they are only available in build or debug
images. Adopt image scanning to detect unexpected binaries. Consider applying seccomp, AppArmor, or SELinux profiles to
restrict available syscalls and reduce the impact of built-in tools. Use ephemeral debug containers (`kubectl debug`)
for troubleshooting instead of shipping troubleshooting utilities in production images. Regularly audit images for drift
to ensure that operational pipelines and base images do not reintroduce unwanted tools.

misconfigurations.name: Hardcoded Credentials in Image Build Specs
misconfigurations.description:
Dockerfiles, CI/CD pipeline scripts, or build specification files that embed secrets leak those values into build
logs, layer history, and artifact metadata. Attackers with read-only access to the images, CI logs, or build environments can extract these
credentials even if they are removed from the last image layer. Such secrets may grant privileged access to code repositories,
artifact registries, or cloud environments. Build-time exposures often remain undetected for long periods.
misconfigurations.impact:
Read-only access to images allows for recovering hardcoded authentication materials. Attackers can use them to
impersonate CI systems, tamper with source code, access private repositories, escalate privileges within the environment or laterally move to cloud.
Build systems may be compromised, enabling widespread injection attacks. Stolen non-expiring credentials provide persistent access across
multiple stages of the SDLC.
misconfigurations.prerequisites: Read-only access to the image repository
misconfigurations.recommendations:
Store secrets outside the build environment using external secret managers such as HashiCorp Vault
or cloud-native key vault (e.g., AWS SecretsManager). Ensure applications retrieve secrets at runtime and keep them only
in memory, avoiding the filesystem or image layers. Enforce automated secret scanning across CI logs, build contexts
and image layers, and block builds if secrets are detected. Rotate all build-time credentials frequently, revoke long-lived tokens,
and ensure leaked credentials trigger immediate rotation and invalidation. Maintain strict access controls on CI/CD environments
and source code repositories to minimize the chance of credential exposure.

misconfigurations.name: Lax Network Policies
misconfigurations.description:
In the absence of NetworkPolicies, Kubernetes networks behave as a fully open, flat network in which any pod can reach
any other pod, internal or external service. This allows attackers who compromise even a single workload to freely scan service
ports, interact with cloud metadata endpoints, or perform reconnaissance against internal management interfaces. Such
clusters effectively lack network segmentation, making every namespace and workload reachable from every other. This
breakdown of network isolation removes a critical defense-in-depth boundary that Kubernetes expects.
misconfigurations.impact:
Once inside any compromised pod, attackers can pivot to databases, secrets stores, internal APIs, or even control-plane
components reachable on internal addresses. Attackers can also download tools for further exploitation, exfiltrate data,
launch enumeration or credential brute-force attacks against internal APIs. In cloud environments, missing or lax network policies
can allow for access to IMDS endpoints, enabling unauthorized access to the cloud account and facilitating privilege escalation
within the Kubernetes cluster.
misconfigurations.prerequisites: Code execution in a pod via any of the `workload_code_execution` techniques
misconfigurations.recommendations:
Implement default-deny Network Policies for both ingress and egress across all namespaces. Explicitly authorize only
required communications. Block access to metadata endpoints and control-plane internal addresses. Regularly audit
namespaces that are missing or bypassing network isolation rules. Ensure that pods do not run as `privileged` or with `hostNetwork: true`
as these configurations allow the pod to ignore any Network Policies. If workloads require these permissions, they must be segregated
onto separate, tightly monitored node group that is not reachable from the untrusted IP ranges.

misconfigurations.name: Encryption at Rest is Not Enabled
misconfigurations.description:
When ETCD is not configured with encryption-at-rest, all stored Kubernetes Secrets and sensitive API objects are written
to disk in plaintext. Any attacker who gains filesystem access to a control-plane node (e.g., through a node compromise,
backup leak, or misconfigured hostPath) can immediately extract service account tokens, TLS private keys, cloud
credentials, and application secrets. This bypasses all RBAC and pod-level isolation because secret confidentiality
collapses entirely once an attacker reaches ETCD’s storage volume. Lightweight Kubernetes distributions such as k3s
exacerbate this issue, because they often use a single-file embedded datastore (`/var/lib/rancher/k3s/k3s.db`) that is
also unencrypted unless explicitly configured, making it easier to access or exfiltrate. Lack of encryption also facilitates
Unauthorized object creation as described in `ETCD Abuse` techniques.
misconfigurations.impact:
Attackers can impersonate any ServiceAccount, extract all Secrets, and reconstruct every credential, token,
or certificate within the cluster. They can also generate arbitrary new identities, forge privileged bindings, or create
backdoored workloads by writing directly to ETCD. Because ETCD and k3s datastores contain the raw authoritative state of
the cluster, compromise of these files enables immediate and total cluster takeover with no need to exploit API
permissions. Even partial leakage (such as a single snapshot) may be enough to fully compromise the environment.
Plaintext storage therefore turns any local host breach, backup leak, or misconfigured volume mount into a
control-plane-level incident.

_or: Filesystem-level access to ETCD data directories or k3s datastore files
Possession of a snapshot from backups, storage plugins, or node volumes.
Valid credentials that allow for authenticating to ETCD over the network

misconfigurations.recommendations:
Enable encryption-at-rest for ETCD using strong keys stored in a hardware-backed KMS or HSM, and ensure key rotation is
enforced as part of cluster maintenance. For k3s, adopt external datastore backends or enable encryption hooks to
avoid leaving `k3s.db` in plaintext. Restrict shell or SSH access to control-plane nodes, enforce disk encryption, and
tightly control access to backups and node volumes that may contain ETCD snapshots. Monitor for unusual access to ETCD
directories and ensure operational tooling never stores snapshots unencrypted. Prevent hostPath access to control-plane
volumes under all circumstances.

misconfigurations.name: Containers with Automounted Service Account Tokens
misconfigurations.description:
When ServiceAccount tokens are automounted into every pod by default, workloads receive API credentials they may not
need. Attackers compromising these workloads can steal tokens and authenticate directly to the Kubernetes API server.
Even if the token only grants minimal permissions, it provides a reliable foothold for reconnaissance or privilege
escalation. Many real-world clusters unintentionally allow broad read access through default SA tokens. Disabling
automounting where unnecessary significantly reduces attack surface.
misconfigurations.impact:
Token theft enables direct interaction with the cluster’s control plane. Weak default ServiceAccounts may
reveal sensitive metadata, resource layouts, or even allow modifying cluster components. Stolen tokens can be replayed
from outside the cluster if the API server is externally reachable.

_and: Network access to the Kube-API server
Code execution in a pod via any `workload_code_execution` technique

misconfigurations.recommendations:
Disable automounting for pods that do not require API access. Use workload-specific ServiceAccounts with strictly
minimal permissions. Audit for automountServiceAccountToken misconfigurations. Rotate and scope ServiceAccounts
effectively.

misconfigurations.name: Hardcoded Credentials in IMDS Startup Scripts
misconfigurations.description: EC2 instances often rely on user-data or cloud-init startup scripts to bootstrap workloads, configure agents, or deploy applications. When these scripts contain hardcoded passwords, API keys, tokens, or long-lived cloud credentials, the secrets become accessible through multiple exposure paths. Startup scripts are stored in instance metadata, configuration management systems, AMIs, backups, and infrastructure-as-code repositories. Any party able to access the instance metadata service (IMDS), snapshots, logs, or the original provisioning artifacts can recover these credentials.
misconfigurations.impact: An attacker with access to the EC2 Instance Metadata Service (IMDS), compromised workloads on the instance, or read access to infrastructure repositories can extract hardcoded credentials from startup scripts. These credentials may grant access to cloud APIs, CI/CD systems, databases, or third-party services. Since startup scripts are frequently reused across environments, compromised credentials can enable lateral movement across accounts, persistence beyond instance lifetime, privilege escalation in cloud environments, and long-term access due to missing rotation or revocation.

_or: Ability to access EC2 IMDS from a workload or the host
Read access to AMIs, snapshots, backups, or infrastructure-as-code repositories containing startup scripts
Compromise of an EC2 instance or container running on the instance

misconfigurations.recommendations: Avoid embedding secrets in user-data or cloud-init scripts. Use IAM roles and temporary credentials obtained via IMDS instead of static credentials wherever possible. Retrieve secrets at runtime from managed secret stores such as AWS Secrets Manager or AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store with encryption enabled. Restrict IMDS access using IMDSv2 and hop limits, and block pod-level access to IMDS where not required. Implement automated scanning for secrets in infrastructure-as-code, user-data templates, AMIs, and configuration repositories. Rotate and revoke any credentials found to be exposed immediately, and enforce short-lived credentials with strict least-privilege policies.

misconfigurations.name: Kube-API Server Accessible from a Broad IP Range
misconfigurations.description:
Exposing the Kubernetes API server to broad CIDR ranges (especially the Internet) removes critical network-based
protections and allows an attacker anywhere in the world to perform authentication attempts or API probing. Exposed
Kube-API server endpoints facilitate lateral movement into Kubernetes after exploiting other vulnerabilities (e.g., an attacker can exploit
a Local File Inclusion vulnerability to read a ServiceAccount token and then use the token to access Kubernetes APIs).
In addition, stolen kubeconfigs or leaked tokens become much more dangerous because they can be used from
any location. Broad API exposure transforms the API server into a public-facing attack surface.
misconfigurations.impact:
Network access to the Kube-API server endpoints facilitates lateral movement into Kubernetes after exploiting
other vulnerabilities. Leaked authentication materials (e.g., Service account tokens, certificates, kubeconfig files,
kubelet bootstrap tokens) can be abused by external attackers to enumerate, gain code execution in containers, or even to
completely compromise the cluster. The authentication materials can be obtained in a number of ways, e.g., exploiting SSRf or
Local File Inclusion, unauthorized access to code repositories or credential vaults, infostealer logs etc.

_and: Possession of a valid authentication material
RBAC permissions to enumerate or modify cluster resources

misconfigurations.recommendations:
Restrict API server access to internal networks, VPNs, or private endpoints. Disable anonymous access and require MFA
for changing the IP range allowlist if possible. Enable audit logging and alert on external API usage. Prefer cloud-native private API
endpoints to completely remove external access paths.

misconfigurations.name: User Namespace Remapping Disabled
misconfigurations.description: Container runtimes support user namespace remapping (`userns-remap`) to map container root users to non-root users on the host. When this feature is not enabled, processes running as root inside a container are also mapped to root on the host from the kernel’s perspective. This collapses a critical isolation boundary between containers and the host operating system. Many container escape techniques rely on this misconfiguration to translate in-container privileges directly into host-level impact.
misconfigurations.impact: If an attacker gains code execution as root inside a container, the absence of user namespace remapping allows the attacker to interact with host resources using real root privileges. This significantly lowers the barrier for container escape, exploitation of kernel vulnerabilities, abuse of writable host mounts, and manipulation of device files. Successful exploitation can lead to full node compromise, persistence on the host, lateral movement to other workloads, and escalation to cluster-wide or cloud-level compromise.

_or: Code execution as root inside a container
Deployment of a privileged or misconfigured container
Ability to exploit a container runtime or kernel vulnerability from within a container

misconfigurations.recommendations: Enable user namespace remapping (`userns-remap`) in the container runtime to ensure container root users are mapped to unprivileged host users. Combine user namespace isolation with dropping unnecessary Linux capabilities, enforcing seccomp and AppArmor or SELinux profiles, and avoiding privileged containers. Ensure host filesystem mounts are strictly read-only where required and avoid exposing sensitive device files to containers. Regularly audit container runtime configurations across all nodes and validate that security hardening settings are consistently applied. Test workloads for compatibility with user namespaces before production rollout.

misconfigurations.name: Missing Authentication on Kubelet Endpoints
misconfigurations.description:
Clusters that expose kubelet APIs (`10250`, `10255`) without proper authentication or TLS enforcement allow
attackers to list pods, view logs, and read credentials, hardcoded into pods' manifests. It may also be possible to execute
OS commands via kubelets' /run or /exec endpoints. Attackers can target any pod on the node regardless of namespace or RBAC
restrictions. This creates a parallel administrative path into the cluster.
misconfigurations.impact:
Attackers may read secrets, pivot into containers, or dump environment variables from any pod. Sensitive logs and
runtime details become accessible. Kubelet exec-style access may provide full container takeover. Node-level
reconnaissance and post-exploitation chains become trivial.

_and: Network access to a Kubelet API server

misconfigurations.recommendations:
Enforce TLS and authentication on all kubelet endpoints. Block access from untrusted networks. Disable read-only ports
audit kubelet request patterns. Use firewall rules or security groups to restrict access to cluster operators only.

misconfigurations.name: Containers Running as Root
misconfigurations.description:
Containers running as root inherit powerful Linux capabilities, unrestricted filesystem permissions, and access to
sensitive kernel interfaces that unprivileged workloads cannot reach. This greatly expands the range of actions an
attacker can take after gaining code execution inside the workload: they can interact with `/proc`, `/sys`, and
device nodes, manipulate file ownership, and access privileged mount points. Root containers also facilitate hostPath mounts abuse
when a sensitive folder was mounted from a node (e.g., `etc` or `sys`). Many container breakout exploits - such as those
abusing cgroups, namespace transitions, or kernel bugs - require UID 0 inside the container to be reliably weaponized. As a result, a single
compromised root container represents an escalation path toward node compromise or cross-container lateral movement.
misconfigurations.impact:
Attackers can leverage root inside the container to create memory dumps of any process in the container and read or
modify any file within the container, including configuration files, application source code, or services that run in the background.
Root-level access facilitates tool installation, data exfiltration, and further enumeration of the environment from within the container.
Also, root-level access allows an attacker to reliably exploit sensitive hostPath mounts and perform operations that
depend on privileged syscalls. Kernel-level vulnerabilities and container escape CVEs become significantly
easier to exploit when executed from a root context.
misconfigurations.prerequisites: Code execution in a pod via any of the `workload_code_execution` techniques
misconfigurations.recommendations:
Enforce non-root users across all container images and block workloads without explicit user settings through policy
engines or PSA restricted mode. Adopt SELinux and seccomp profiles to limit which files are accessible and which syscalls containers can
perform, ensuring that even root workloads face mandatory access controls. In Docker-based environments, enable
userns-remap to map container root to an unprivileged UID on the host, reducing breakout risk.
In Kubernetes-based environments, use the "user namespaces" feature. Avoid granting CAP_SYS_ADMIN, CAP_NET_ADMIN,
and other powerful capabilities unless absolutely necessary. Continuously audit for UID 0 usage and ensure that
operational pipelines cannot reintroduce root defaults into production workloads.

misconfigurations.name: Lax Resource Consumption Limits
misconfigurations.description:
Without CPU, memory, and ephemeral storage limits, a single compromised container can monopolize node resources and
starve neighboring workloads. Attackers can deliberately trigger out-of-memory (OOM) conditions, eviction storms, or
node-pressure scenarios that destabilize the entire cluster. Excessive resource usage also drives autoscaling
misbehavior, potentially creating unnecessary nodes or exhausting quotas. Attackers may use resource pressure to force
pods to reschedule onto attacker-preferred nodes. This subtly enables lateral movement through eviction-based
scheduling manipulation.
misconfigurations.impact:
Critical workloads may be evicted, degraded, or permanently starved. Attackers can force workloads to migrate, disrupt
availability, or undermine horizontal pod autoscaling. Unbounded ephemeral storage can disable node logging,
monitoring, or system daemons. Node stability degrades quickly under resource-intensive attacks. In addition, unconstrainted
resource usage also drives autoscaling misbehavior, potentially creating unnecessary nodes or exhausting quotas which inflates
infrastructure costs.
misconfigurations.prerequisites: Code execution in a pod via any of the `workload_code_execution` techniques
misconfigurations.recommendations:
Enforce LimitRanges and ResourceQuotas to constrain CPU, memory, and storage consumption. Ensure critical namespaces
have conservative limits to prevent noisy-neighbor effects. Monitor nodes for resource spikes and correlate with pod
restarts and evictions. Use autoscaler safeguards to avoid runaway provisioning.

misconfigurations.name: Hardcoded Credentials in Kubernetes Deployment Manifests
misconfigurations.description: Kubernetes deployment manifests frequently define environment variables, command-line arguments, or configuration values required by applications at runtime. When these manifests embed hardcoded credentials such as passwords, API keys, tokens, or certificates directly into Pod, Deployment, StatefulSet, or DaemonSet specifications, the secrets become part of the cluster’s declarative configuration. These credentials are often stored in version control systems, CI/CD pipelines, artifact repositories, and backups, and may be visible to any principal with read access to workload manifests. Hardcoding credentials in manifests bypasses Kubernetes secret management mechanisms and significantly increases the risk of widespread credential exposure.
misconfigurations.impact: An attacker with read access to Kubernetes workload manifests, access to the Kubernetes API, or compromise of a CI/CD system can extract hardcoded credentials directly from deployment specifications. These credentials may grant access to internal services, databases, message queues, cloud APIs, or third-party systems. Because manifests are frequently reused across namespaces, clusters, and environments, leaked credentials can enable lateral movement, impersonation of applications, escalation to higher-privilege workloads, or compromise of external infrastructure. Once exposed, these credentials are difficult to fully revoke due to manifest propagation, cached configurations, and long-lived tokens, often resulting in persistent access for the attacker.

_and: Permissions to `get` / `list` / `watch` the affected kubernetes resource
Network access to the Kubernetes API server or access to the source repository containing the manifests.

misconfigurations.recommendations: Never embed credentials directly in Kubernetes manifests. Use Kubernetes Secrets or external secret management systems such as AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, or cloud-native key vaults, and inject secrets at runtime using environment variables or volume mounts. Enforce automated secret scanning on infrastructure-as-code repositories, CI/CD pipelines, and rendered manifests before deployment. Apply strict RBAC controls to limit who can read workload specifications, and avoid granting list permissions on workloads to non-administrative principals. Rotate and revoke any credentials discovered in manifests immediately, and prefer short-lived credentials and workload identity mechanisms to reduce blast radius.

misconfigurations.name: Lack of Mutual TLS (mTLS) Between Pods
misconfigurations.description:
When inter-pod traffic is not protected with mutual TLS, workloads implicitly trust the network and exchange data
in plaintext or with unauthenticated encryption. This makes all service-to-service communication vulnerable to passive
interception and active manipulation by any workload that gains the ability to observe or redirect traffic. Without
mTLS, pods cannot verify the identity of their peers, allowing attackers to impersonate services, spoof endpoints, or
inject altered responses into service meshes. Many Kubernetes networks rely solely on IP-based identity or namespace
separation, neither of which provides any cryptographic assurance.
misconfigurations.impact:
Attackers can perform credential theft, session hijacking, and protocol manipulation by intercepting or tampering with
unprotected traffic between services. Sensitive data (e.g., API keys, OAuth tokens, database credentials, or
application secrets) may be exposed in transit. The lack of mTLS enables every attack in the
`Adversary-in-the-Middle Against Workloads` category to succeed with minimal friction, including ARP spoofing, service hijacking,
CoreDNS poisoning, and IP/endpoint spoofing. The trust boundary collapses because nothing validates the identities of
communicating services.
misconfigurations.prerequisites: Adjacent access to the cluster network
misconfigurations.recommendations:
Enforce mutual TLS for all inter-service communication using a service mesh on top of eBPF, such as Cilium.
Require certificate-based workload identity so each service can authenticate its peers.
Reject plaintext or unauthenticated traffic through admission policies and application-level controls. Regularly audit
namespaces and services to ensure that mTLS is enabled and enforced cluster-wide. Combine mTLS with NetworkPolicies to
limit lateral movement and block traffic from unauthorized workloads.

misconfigurations.name: Hardcoded Credentials on Kubernetes Nodes
misconfigurations.description: Kubernetes worker and control-plane nodes can contain hardcoded credentials introduced during manual administration, debugging, bootstrapping, or incident response. Operators may export cloud credentials, API tokens, database passwords, or service secrets directly into shell environments, scripts, or configuration files on the node. These credentials can persist unintentionally in shell history files, language-specific REPL histories (e.g., Python), temporary scripts, or home directories. Once written to disk, such secrets frequently may survive reboots, user sessions, and even partial node remediation, creating a long-lived exposure surface on the host.
misconfigurations.impact: An attacker who achieves node-level access—through container escape, privileged workload abuse, or compromised SSH access can recover hardcoded credentials left behind by administrators or automation. These credentials may grant access to cloud control planes, container registries, CI/CD systems, internal services, or other Kubernetes clusters. Because node access often implies high trust, the discovered credentials are frequently over-privileged and long-lived, enabling rapid lateral movement, persistence beyond node lifetime, escalation to cluster-admin, or full cloud account compromise. Credential exposure on nodes is particularly dangerous because it is rarely monitored, difficult to audit retroactively, and often overlooked during incident response.

_or: Code execution on the node via container escape or privileged workload
Read access to node filesystems via hostPath mounts, runtime socket abuse, or /var/log abuse

misconfigurations.recommendations: Never store credentials directly on Kubernetes nodes. Use workload identity mechanisms, IAM roles, and short-lived credentials instead of static secrets. Enforce strict operational hygiene: avoid exporting secrets into shells, remove ad-hoc scripts after use, and disable persistent shell histories where possible. Regularly scan nodes for known credential patterns and sensitive files, and include node home directories in incident response playbooks. Rotate and revoke any credentials discovered on nodes immediately. Limit node access to a minimal set of trusted operators, and prefer immutable node images with automated provisioning over manual configuration.

misconfigurations.name: Lax Pod Security Admission Settings
misconfigurations.description:
Weak or misconfigured Pod Security Admission (PSA) allows workloads to request privileged fields such as running as
root, using hostPath, enabling privileged mode, or entering host namespaces. PSA exemptions or namespaces configured
in “privileged” mode create blind spots where dangerous workloads can run freely. Attackers exploiting RCE in such
workloads gain immediate access to escalated capabilities. PSA misconfiguration neutralizes core cluster isolation
guarantees. It significantly increases the success rate of host-escape and privilege-escalation attacks.
misconfigurations.impact:
Attackers may escape containers, access host filesystems, or manipulate sensitive volumes. Security boundaries
collapse, allowing escalation from workload compromise into node compromise. HostPath abuse and privileged-mode
misuse become trivial. Techniques such as `Deploy Container via Workload Controllers` can be abused to achieve cluster-wide RCE on every node.

_and: Network access to the Kube-API server
Possessing any permissions described in the `Deploy Container via Workload Controllers` technique.

misconfigurations.recommendations:
Enforce Pod Security Admission in every non-privileged namespace that hosts customer-facing workloads.
Ensure that the setting is `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce = restricted` to disallow privileged containers,
host-path mounts, container running as root, hostPID, hostIPC, and hostNetwork. This effectively prevents host escape
vectors through privileged Kubernetes containers. Regularly audit all namespaces for PSA drift or privilege creep.
